description: Cold hands slide against each other until friction warms them. 'rohi' keeps surfaces moving together under pressure. It can warm skin, polish wood, work oil in, or ease an ache. 'hewe' (wipe) moves across a surface to clear material; 'palo' (touch) needs contact but no sliding.
articulatory_notes: Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge, then round the lips for stressed middle-height back 'o'. Open the glottis for unvoiced 'h', let breath pass freely, and raise the tongue into high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The opening tap makes one quick contact, and a short trill repeats it. Rounded 'o' then opens into airy 'h' before high-front 'i', a contact-and-release pattern that suits repeated strokes.
usage_notes: The surface or body part being rubbed stands before the verb. 'roe' can introduce a hand, cloth, or tool, while material worked into a surface takes 'muo' (INTO). As a noun, 'rohi' is rubbing or frictional work.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Friction fire and hand polishing both depend on repeated contact under pressure. 'rohi' keeps the body's work visible beside the result.

semantic_domains.activity: working through repeated or sustained sliding contact
semantic_domains.physical: friction produced by pressured movement across a surface
